edge: Local features need to be invariant to imaging variations for establishing correspondences between images.
The framework is used to extract discriminative rotation-invariant descriptors.
The method uses group-equivariant CNNs to learn rotation-equivariant features.
The orientations are processed by group aligning technique to achieve rotation-invariance.
The method demonstrates state-of-the-art matching accuracy among existing rotation-invariant descriptors.
The method shows competitive results when transferred to the task of keypoint matching.
The method also shows competitive results when transferred to the task of camera pose estimation.
Local descriptors are essential for establishing visual correspondence across images.
Local descriptors play a crucial role in visual localization.
Local descriptors are used in simultaneous localization and mapping.
Local descriptors are also important for 3D reconstruction.
Local descriptors need to possess properties of invariance.
Local descriptors also need to be discriminative to distinguish true matches.
Deep neural networks have been adopted to learn local descriptors for enhanced performance.
Achieving rotation invariance without sacrificing discriminativeness is important for local descriptors.
Rotation is a common imaging variation that needs to be addressed in local descriptors.
This work proposes a self-supervised approach for local descriptors.
group-equivariant CNNs are used to jointly extract rotation-equivariant local features and their orientations from an image in the proposed self-supervised approach.
The orientation alignment loss trains the network to predict the dominant orientation robustly against imaging variations such as illumination or viewpoint changes.

Group-aligning shifts the group-equivariant features by their dominant orientation along their group dimension to obtain discriminative rotation-invariant descriptors from rotation-equivariant features.
Conventional methods to yield invariant features from group-equivariant features collapse the group dimension by group-pooling, resulting in a drop in feature discriminability and quality.
Our group-aligning technique preserves the group dimension, achieving rotation-invariance while maintaining discriminability of the features.
The rotation-invariant local descriptors are proposed to tackle the task of visual correspondence by utilizing rotation-equivariant CNNs.

Group-aligning is a method proposed to shift a group-equivariant descriptor in the group dimension by its dominant orientation to obtain a rotation-invariant descriptor without collapsing the group information.
Self-supervisory losses include orientation alignment loss for orientation estimation to ensure robust local descriptor extraction.
Self-supervisory losses also involve a contrastive descriptor loss for robust local descriptor extraction.
State-of-the-art performances are demonstrated under varying rotations on the Roto-360 dataset.
Competitive transferability is shown on the HPatches dataset.
Competitive transferability is demonstrated on the MVS dataset.

Classical methods extract features by first aggregating image gradients to obtain a rotation-equivariant representation, from which the estimated dominant orientation is subtracted to obtain rotation-invariant features.
A proposal is made to obtain orientation-normalized local descriptors through a deep end-to-end pipeline utilizing rotation-equivariant CNNs with additional losses.
GIFT constructs group-equivariant features by rotating or rescaling images and then collapses the group dimension using bilinear pooling to obtain invariant local descriptors.
LISRD jointly learns meta descriptors with different levels of regional variations.
LISRD [42] jointly learns meta descriptors with different levels of regional variations and selects the most appropriate level of invariance given the context.
Another branch of learning methods aims to learn the invariance implicitly using descriptor similarity losses from the image pair using camera pose or homography supervision.

We construct group-equivariant local features using the steerable networks [60], which explicitly encodes cyclic rotational equivariance to the features without having to rely on data augmentation.
We construct group-equivariant local features using the steerable networks [60], which explicitly encodes cyclic rotational equivariance to the features without having to rely on data augmentation.

We can then yield rotation-invariant features by group-aligning that shifts the group-equivariant features along the group dimension by their dominant orientations, preserving feature discriminability.
There has been a constant pursuit to learn equivariant representations by explicitly incorporating group equivariance into the model architecture design [30–32, 51, 60, 65].
There has been a constant pursuit to learn equivariant representations by explicitly incorporating group equivariance into the model architecture design [30–32, 51, 60, 65].
For example, G-CNNs [6] use group equivariant convolutions that reduce sample complexity by exploiting symmetries on discrete isometric groups.
SFCNNs [61] and H-Nets [62] extract features from more diverse groups and continuous domains by using harmonics as ﬁlters.

Descriptor similarity loss motivated by contrastive learning.
Descriptor loss formulated in a contrastive manner following contrastive learning.
Unlike the triplet loss with one hard negative sample, the contrastive loss can optimize the distance for all negative pairs.
Contrastive loss with InfoNCE maximizes the mutual information between the encoded features and effectively reduces the low-level noise.
Overall self-supervised loss formulated as L = ↵Lori + Ldesc, where ↵ is a balancing term.
